coredata.dc:title: Modulation of neurosecretion and approaches for its multistep analysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA) - General Subjects
coredata.dc:creator: Kasatkina, Ludmila A.
Gumenyuk, Vitaliy P.
Sturm, Eva M.
Heinemann, Akos
Bernas, Tytus
Trikash, Irene O.
coredata.dc:description:
Background Neurosecretion is the multistep process occurring in separate spatial and temporal cellular boundaries which complicates its comprehensive analysis. Most of the research are focused on one distinct stage of synaptic vesicle recycling. Here, we describe approaches for complex analysis of synaptic vesicle (SV) endocytosis and separate steps of exocytosis at the level of presynaptic bouton and highly purified SVs.
Methods Proposed fluorescence-based strategies and analysis of neurotransmitter transport provided the advantages in studies of exocytosis steps. We evaluated SV docking/tethering, their Ca2+-dependent fusion and release of neurotransmitters gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and glutamate in two animal models.
Results Approaches enabled us to study: 1) endocytosis/Ca2+-dependent release of fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) during stimulation of nerve terminals; 2) the action of levetiracetam, modulator of SV glycoprotein SV2, on fusion competence of SVs and stimulated release of GABA and glutamate; 3) impairments of several steps of neurosecretion under vitamin D3 deficiency.
Conclusions Our algorithm enabled us to verify the method validity for multidimensional analysis of SV turnover. By increasing SV docking and the size of readily releasable pool (RRP), levetiracetam is able to selectively enhance the stimulated GABA secretion in hippocampal neurons. Findings suggest that SV2 regulates RRP through impact on the number of docked/primed SVs.
General significance Methodology can be widely applied to study the stimulated neurosecretion in presynapse, regulation of SV docking, their Ca2+-dependent fusion with target membranes, quantitative analysis of expression of neuron-specific proteins, as well as for testing the efficiency of pre-selected designed neuroactive substances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synaptic vesicles recycling
Confocal imaging
Carbon nanodots
Vitamin D3 deficiency
SV2 protein
GABA release

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Results
Visualization of CNDs endocytosis in isolated presynaptic nerve terminals
Exocytosis of fluorescent CNDs in isolated nerve terminals
Blocking of endocytotic CND loading in hippocampal neurons
Registration of SV cluster formation by electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering
Functional state of isolated SVs and recycling in living neurons
Tracking of endo−/exocytosis and its modulation by levetiracetam
Impairments of synaptic vesicle docking and fusion under vitamin D3 deficiency
FACS analysis of surface and intracellular neuron-specific proteins
Conclusions
Materials and methods
Materials
Ethics statement
Animal models
Isolation of synaptosomes preparation from rat brain
Isolation of synaptic vesicles and cytosolic fraction of synaptosomes
Primary cultures of cortical neurons
Transmission electron microscopy of the isolated synaptic vesicles
Dynamic light scattering for monitoring SV clustering and CNDs sizes
R18 assay for membrane fusion monitoring
Visualization of stimulated uptake and release of CNDs in presynapse
Flow cytometry analysis of endocytotic CND loading in hippocampal neurons
Confocal imaging of stimulated SV fusion in preformed clusters
Visualization of SV2A: immunocytochemistry on primary hippocampal neurons
Registration of the synaptic vesicles acidification and exocytotic release
Estimation of GABA release
FM1-43 staining of recycling synaptic vesicles
Fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) analysis: intracellular staining
Statistical analysis
References

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Fund for Fundamental Research of Ukraine
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Fund for Fundamental Research of Ukraine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by OPTEC's grant program (project No. 6-13, 2013 ), by grant No. F63/41-2016 of the State Fund for Fundamental Research of Ukraine (SFFRU) and by joint Ukraine-Austria R&D project (No. UA 09/2016 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Imaging of CNDs loading and release in presynapse. R18-labeled isolated rat brain nerve terminals (synaptosomes) were loaded with CNDs during high-K+ stimulation in Ca2+-containing solution (5 min, 30 °C). Epifluorescent images of synaptosomes (A) and their slices obtained in Z-stack scanning mode (B and C). D, slices of R18-labeled synaptosomes after high-K+ stimulated CNDs release. Fluorescence intensity profiles of R18 and CND emission in randomly selected synaptosomes marked by numerals on C and D. Typical images were captured by digital integrated camera AxioCam for 7 independent preparations. The excitation for CNDs and R18 was 405 nm and 543 nm, respectively. Scale bar is 2 μm (A), 2 μm (B), 5 μm (C) and 2 μm (D). E, excitation and emission spectra of R18 and CNDs. F, flow cytometry histograms of the unstimulated loading and high-K+ stimulated uptake of CNDs in hippocampal neurons in the presence of endocytosis inhibitor dynasore (80 μM). Loading conditions for traces are marked by corresponding colours. Analysis was based on the count of 30,000 events.

The functional state and visualization of isolated SVs in cell-free system and in living neurons. Electron micrographs of isolated SVs in standard buffered solution (A) or in the presence of cytosolic synaptosomal proteins, 1 mg/mL (B). C, size distributions of isolated SVs in the absence (purple line), presence (red line) of cytosolic synaptosomal proteins and after application of 10 μM Ca2+ (blue line). Traces are typical for 7 independent experiments. D, scatter plot of the SV distributions in the absence or presence of synaptosomal cytosolic proteins and Ca2+. The sizes of single SVs and docked SV clusters (SVC) were assessed by dynamic light scattering. Histogram is typical for 7 independent experiments. E, the acidification of isolated SVs registered with pH-sensitive fluorescent dye AO and the dissipation of the proton gradient upon application of protonophore FCCP (1 μM). F, the visualization of FM1-43-labeled SVs in primary cortical neurons and depolarization-induced FM1-43 destaining. Scale bar is 50 μm. To exclude the spontaneous exocytosis the steady-state fluorescence was registered before stimulation and in standard solution (red trace). FM1-43 destaining was calculated in percentage of total accumulated dye. Traces are typical for 5 independent experiments.

Modulation of sequential events of neurosecretion by antiepileptic drug levetiracetam. A, stimulated AO release from primary neurons in the presence of levetiracetam (100 μM) or after blockage of T-type Ca2+-channels (1 mM ethosuximide). Exocytosis was stimulated by the application of high-K+ (35 mM K+ final) in Ca2+-supplemented medium for the duration indicated by the horizontal bars. Starts of synchronous/asynchronous exocytosis and endocytosis are indicated. AO intensity profiles were normalized to the similar traces at the absence of synaptosomes/primary neurons. Traces are typical for 10 independent experiments. B, visualization of SV2A as target of levetiracetam on SVs: immunocytochemistry of primary neurons (14 DIC). Images were captured by transmitted light (left panel) and by two separate channels for DAPI and Alexa-Fluor 555 fluorescence (right panel). C, clustering of isolated SVs in the presence of levetiracetam (100 μM). The sizes of SVs and SV clusters were assessed by dynamic light scattering in the medium of cytosolic synaptic proteins. Histogram is typical for 7 independent experiments. D–F, analysis of secretion in animal model of levetiracetam administration (14 days, 60 mg/kg per os, once a day): D, stimulated secretion in cortical and hippocampal neurons; E, estimation of readily releasable pool of SVs in hippocampal neurons. Traces are typical for 6 independent experiments. F, Stimulated release of [14C]GABA and l-glutamate from hippocampal neurons. Release was induced by high-K+ depolarization in Ca2+-supplemented medium, n = 8.

Stimulated secretion in isolated brain nerve terminals (synaptosomes) under vitamin D3 deficiency. A, exocytotic release of AO in synaptosomes (0.2 mg/mL protein) of control (black) and VDD (red) animals. Exocytosis was stimulated by high-K+ depolarization (35 mM K+ final) in Ca2+-supplemented medium. Normalized curves are typical for 7 independent experiments. B, parameters of development of VDD in experimental model. Values are means ± SD for n = 8. Cholesterol level was assessed by gas chromatography on HRGC 5300, Carlo Elba Instruments (glass column 0.5 m, packed with 1.5% OV-1 on 80–100 mesh Chimalite) at 250 °C. C, size distributions of particles in suspension of purified SVs in the presence of synaptosomal cytosolic proteins: red - VDD, green - control animals. D, Ca2+-triggered fusion of SV membranes in the presence of synaptosomal cytosolic proteins: SVs and cytosolic proteins were isolated from brain nerve terminals of control (black) and VDD (red) animals. The traces are typical for 8 independent experiments. The membrane fusion was initiated by application of 10−5 М Са2+. Traces are average for 8 independent experiments. Right panel: confocal imaging of fused SV in preformed clusters assayed with R18 probe. Scale bar is 50 μm. Free Ca2+ concentration was adjusted with Ca2+/EGTA buffer.

Fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) analysis: intracellular staining of neuron-specific proteins. A, mouse cortex single cells were treated with FIX&PERM (Nordic MUBio), and then stained with TubIIIβ (clone AA10) Alexa Fluor® 488 (filled histogram, Biolegend) or mouse IgG2a, κ Alexa Fluor® 488 isotype control (open histogram, Biolegend) for 30 min. Samples were analyzed on a BD FACSCanto II. B, mouse hippocampal single cells were treated with FIX&PERM (Nordic MUBio), and then stained with TubIIIβ (clone AA10) Alexa Fluor® 488 (filled histogram, Biolegend) or mouse IgG2a, κ Alexa Fluor® 488 isotype control (open histogram, Biolegend) for 30 min. Samples were analyzed on a BD FACSCanto II.
Reconstruction of endo- and exocytosis: the action of levetiracetam on synaptic vesicle recycling and neurotransmitter release.

head.ce:title: Modulation of neurosecretion and approaches for its multistep analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Department of Neurochemistry, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, NAS of Ukraine, 9 Leontovycha Street, 01030 Kyiv, Ukraine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Otto-Loewi Research Center, Pharmacology Section, Medical University of Graz, Universitätsplatz 4, 8010 Graz, Austria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nencki Institute of Experimental Biology, Laboratory of Imaging Tissue Structure and Function, 3 Pasteur Street, 02-093 Warsaw, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Imaging Tissue Structure and Function

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Neurochemistry, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, NAS of Ukraine, 9 Leontovycha str., Kyiv, Ukraine.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Neurosecretion is the multistep process occurring in separate spatial and temporal cellular boundaries which complicates its comprehensive analysis. Most of the research are focused on one distinct stage of synaptic vesicle recycling. Here, we describe approaches for complex analysis of synaptic vesicle (SV) endocytosis and separate steps of exocytosis at the level of presynaptic bouton and highly purified SVs.

Proposed fluorescence-based strategies and analysis of neurotransmitter transport provided the advantages in studies of exocytosis steps. We evaluated SV docking/tethering, their Ca2+-dependent fusion and release of neurotransmitters gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and glutamate in two animal models.

Approaches enabled us to study: 1) endocytosis/Ca2+-dependent release of fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) during stimulation of nerve terminals; 2) the action of levetiracetam, modulator of SV glycoprotein SV2, on fusion competence of SVs and stimulated release of GABA and glutamate; 3) impairments of several steps of neurosecretion under vitamin D3 deficiency.

Our algorithm enabled us to verify the method validity for multidimensional analysis of SV turnover. By increasing SV docking and the size of readily releasable pool (RRP), levetiracetam is able to selectively enhance the stimulated GABA secretion in hippocampal neurons. Findings suggest that SV2 regulates RRP through impact on the number of docked/primed SVs.

Methodology can be widely applied to study the stimulated neurosecretion in presynapse, regulation of SV docking, their Ca2+-dependent fusion with target membranes, quantitative analysis of expression of neuron-specific proteins, as well as for testing the efficiency of pre-selected designed neuroactive substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitter release, a key aspect of neuronal signaling is governed by the life cycle of synaptic vesicles (SVs) which deliver neurotransmitters to the synaptic gap. The axonal transport of SVs in neurons, their transfer between the pools, Ca2+-triggered fusion and recovery during endocytosis determine the establishment, efficacy and functional plasticity of neurotransmitter release [1]. SVs store specific neurotransmitters and undergo Ca2+-triggered exocytosis upon arrival of the action potential at the presynaptic nerve terminal [2].

According to classic model of full collapse fusion by Heuser and Reese [3], vesicle membrane completely merges with the plasma membrane. During endocytosis, that follows exocytosis, SV is being retrieved at a site separate from that of exocytosis. Exocytosis releases neurotransmitters to mediate synaptic transmission, whereas endocytosis recycles vesicles to maintain synaptic activity. Exocytosis and endocytosis are both triggered by depolarization and complete within milliseconds or seconds, which makes these opposite processes hard to distinguish temporarily. Thus, exocytosis and endocytosis are separate events that, primarily, take place at the different subcellular sites.

Although, contemporary improvement of imaging techniques have sufficiently advanced our understanding of the synaptic processes, further developing of less invasive labeling tags is required to assist the exploration of dynamic synaptic mechanisms [4].

SV release is mediated by the array of SNARE-proteins (SNAP (Soluble NSF Attachment Protein) Receptors) that orchestrate the vesicle attachment to the plasma membrane with the following formation of SNARE complex (docking), activation of the fusion machinery (priming) and neurotransmitter release [5, 6].

Certain steps of SNARE-mediated fusion are strictly Ca2+-dependent. After calcium binding to synaptotagmin and rearrangement of the SNARE complex vesicles docked and primed at the active zone enter the fusion step [7]. During the compound exocytosis SVs undergo both homotypic fusion with each other and then heterotypic fusion with plasma membrane [8].

Until recently technical limitations have prevented the detailed investigation and characterization of elementary steps of the exo- and endocytosis due to their fast kinetics, complex regulation, dependence on functional state of SVs, physical and chemical properties of the membrane. Model proposed here describes SV docking and fusion as sequential steps and allows studying the specific mechanisms of endo- and exocytosis. In cell-free system SVs remain fusion competent and retain the proton electrochemical gradient in vitro [9].

Another complication is close temporal overlap between exocytosis and endocytosis. SV turnover can be examined by the tracking of vesicle recycling and single exocytotic event in many cell types [10, 11]. Typical approach employed to detect the synaptic vesicles recycling is the monitoring of internalization of fluorescent styryl dye FM1-43 during endocytosis [12].

Some research groups optimized protocols for specific labeling of neurons and glial cells with functionalized quantum dots and carbon nanodots (CNDs). CNDs are small carbon nanoparticles with various levels of surface passivation that have already been extensively investigated [13–15], with major advances already made in their synthesis, physical properties, biocompatibility and bioapplication [15–17]. We assumed the application of these nanosized structures as novel fluorescent markers of exo−/endocytosis and compared this approach with the other techniques for characterization of SV turnover.

We obtained new data on modulation of SV recycling and transmitter release by antiepileptic drug levetiracetam and impairments of neurosecretion under vitamin D3 deficiency (VDD). VDD is one of the important trigger implicated in development of neurological disorders, i.e. multiple sclerosis, schizophrenia, mental retardation. Methods utilized in our studies confirmed approach validity for analysis of the individual steps of SV life cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Visualization of CNDs endocytosis in isolated presynaptic nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: Isolated brain nerve terminals (synaptosomes) are the presynaptic boutons separated from their axons, with the preserved connection to the postsynaptic membrane of pinched-off dendritic spines. Synaptosomes contain all the machinery necessary for the endocytosis, neurotransmitter uptake and exocytosis. The advantage of synaptosomes is that they can be isolated from adult brain in animal models and allow comparison of exocytosis and endocytotic uptake in different pathologies, during pharmacological studies or other manipulations.

Prior loading with CNDs all preparations of synaptosomes were shown to be functionally active and released the synaptic vesicle constituents via stimulated exocytosis (as in Fig. 3A, control trace). CNDs were loaded into the synaptosomes via depolarization-induced endocytosis (35 mM K+ in Ca2+-supplemented medium). The process was strictly Ca2+-dependent and in the absence of Ca2+ the loading was attenuated by 90% in average. Double washing of the synaptosomal preparation after loading excluded the visible presence of unbound CNDs in the surrounding media.

Synaptosomal membranes were visualized with fluorescent lipophilic probe R18 (Fig. 1A–D) at a concentration that did not cause dye self-quenching. R18 is fully incorporated into the lipid bilayer with the fluorophore at the aqueous interface and the alkyl tail protruding into the lipid interior.

Fig. 1A demonstrates the epifluorescent images of synaptosomes loaded with CNDs. R18 and CND fluorescence was detected in separate channels and on merged images. The slices of R18-labeled CNDs-loaded synaptosomes were shown in Fig. 1B and C. Fig. 1B demonstrates zoomed synaptosome with R18-labeled plasma membrane and CNDs inside.

The registration of CNDs incorporation into the functional nerve terminals was estimated via comparing the intensity profiles of regions of interest with constant acquisition settings. The slices of R18-labeled CNDs-loaded synaptosomes are shown in Fig. 1C with fluorescence profiles indicated below. Profiles correspond to the synaptosomes marked by numerals on merged image.

For R18 the excitation max was 553 nm and emission peak was at 578 nm (Fig. 1E). Excitation maximum for CNDs was 455 nm and emission maximum was 545 nm. To prove the absence of cross-excitation we registered emission of CNDs at excitation 543 nm (laser used for R18 in confocal imaging) and emission of R18 at excitation of 405 nm (laser used to excite CNDs). When excited with 405 nm R18 display low peak of fluorescence. Similarly, CNDs displayed low peak of fluorescence at 543 nm.
Next, preparations of CNDs-loaded synaptosomes were used to detect exocytosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exocytosis of fluorescent CNDs in isolated nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: R18-labeled synaptosomes pre-loaded with CNDs (Fig. 1C) were stimulated by high-K+ (35 mM K+, incubation at 37 °C for 5 min in Ca2+-supplemented medium) to induce the exocytotic release. Synaptosomes were then washed twice with standard salt solution to eliminate all secreted constituents.

Confocal imaging (Fig. 1D) and fluorescence intensity profiles below demonstrated the depletion of CND pool after repeated cycles of exocytosis upon high-K+ stimulation (Fig. 1D). Fluorescence in R18 channel exhibited the boundaries of plasma membrane, while the CND channel represents the residual fluorescence of dots inside the synaptosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blocking of endocytotic CND loading in hippocampal neurons
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the endocytic nature of CNDs uptake we used the loading of hippocampal neurons with CNDs using high-K+ depolarization in Ca2+-supplemented medium and apply the highly efficient inhibitor of SV endocytosis dynasore. Dynasore is a cell-permeable, reversible and non-competitive inhibitor of dynamin 1/2 GTPase activity essential for synaptic vesicle budding.

By flow cytometry we analyzed the fluorescence intensity of CNDs associated with cells (Fig. 1F) in different variations of the experiment: 1. Unlabeled cells (5 min at 37 °C) - autofluorescence (blue trace); 2. Fluorescence of cells after application of CNDs in 2 mM Ca2+ (red trace). This signal was subequal in Ca2+-free and Ca2+-supplemented medium. The difference between this peak and autofluorescence evidences for the step of CND adsorption on plasma membrane. 3. Green trace represents the distribution of the neurons after endocytotic loading of CNDs stimulated with high-K+ (5 min at 37 °C). 4. CNDs loading at the same conditions as in 3, but in the presence of dynasore (80 μM), the inhibitor of dynamin-dependent synaptic vesicle endocytosis - orange trace.
As demonstrated in Fig. 1F, CNDs are initially adsorbed on the plasma membrane, but their stimulated loading is much prominent (118.6 vs 4.12 on fluorescence axis).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Registration of SV cluster formation by electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of SVs to form clusters is essential feature of their fusion competence. Electron micrographs indicated that average diameter of SV is 40–50 nm (Fig. 2A). The formation of SV clusters with the average diameter of 150–300 nm (Fig. 2B) has been shown in the medium with synaptosomal cytosolic proteins. Dynamic light scattering measurements demonstrated that single SVs (Fig. 2C, purple line) form clusters with the size of 450 nm (Fig. 2, red line) and SV clustering further increase in the presence of 10 μM Ca2+ (Fig. 2, blue line), which may be a result of cluster coalescence [18].
The distributions of single SVs and SV clusters ratio in the absence or presence of synaptosomal cytosolic proteins and Ca2+ is demonstrated in Fig. 2D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional state of isolated SVs and recycling in living neurons
ce:section.ce:para: We next aimed to study the correlation between the stimulated release of CNDs with the depolarization-induced secretion of SV constituents in presynapse. Functionally active SV preparation was proved crucial for correct modeling of exocytotic steps in cell-free system. Proton gradient generated by V-type H+-ATPase of isolated SVs was tested with pH-sensitive fluorescent dye AO. Application of protonophore FCCP (1 μM) at the steady-state level of dye fluorescence caused the dissipation of vesicle ∆pH (Fig. 2E). Proton electrochemical gradient of SVs is essential not only for their Ca2+-induced membrane fusion but also for active vesicular neurotransmitter uptake.

The typical approach for monitoring of SV recycling in living cells is staining and destaining of the neuronal membrane structures with FM dyes. Hydrophobic dye FM1-43 is incorporated in plasma membrane and during high-K+ stimulation is trapped by means of endocytosis in newly formed SVs. After repeated cycles of such stimulation the major fraction of SVs within the cells are labeled and their localization and movements can be visualized by confocal imaging (Fig. 2F) or super-resolution microscopy.

Due to the membranotropic properties of the dye one can observe FM1-43 fluorescence in plasma membranes, although the level of this fluorescence is typically lower than that of synaptic vesicle membranes. This is one of the disadvantages of this dye.

Following depolarization-induced exocytosis in nerve terminals FM1-43 was released together with fused SVs and its fluorescence intensity decreased. The calculations of the rate and the level of FM1-43 destaining in nerve terminals were made based on the time-course of the decrease in fluorescence (Fig. 2F). The intensity of FM1-43 fluorescence in loaded neurons without stimulation was defined as 100%. In contrast to CNDs, FM1-43 dequenching during exocytosis allows registering and quantitative analysis of the exocytotic release in spectrofluorimetric time-based measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tracking of endo−/exocytosis and its modulation by levetiracetam
ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitters glutamate, serotonin and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) are accumulated in synaptic vesicles via active transport driven by proton electrochemical gradient. The rate and the intensity of exo−/endocytosis correlate with the kinetics of neurotransmitter transport and can be estimated by the dynamics of pH-sensitive dye acridine orange (AO).

To induce the secretion of SV constituents from synaptosomes and neurons the same stimulation conditions (isosmotic 35 mM K+ in the presence of 2 mM Ca2+ as in Figs. 3A, D, 4A ) were used. As shown by dotted lines in Fig. 3A, upon high-K+ depolarization the first fast exocytosis is observed, which corresponds to the synchronous exocytosis [19] and release of docked/primed SVs (readily releasable pool, RRP) foremost [20]. Endocytosis proceeds rapidly following exocytosis and is registered as the decrease in AO fluorescence due to its reverse accumulation. The normalized fluorescence intensity (Ft/F0) allows quantitative analysis of each trace.

Traces of AO fluorescence intensity represents the dynamics of dye within presynapse that is the summation of its exocytotic release and re-uptake in newly formed endosomes and SVs. Immediately after synchronous exocytosis endocytosis is intensified. Later, due to the predominance of endocytosis the resulting curve is the decrease of AO green fluorescence although asynchronous exocytosis also occurs at this time.

The dynamics of pH-sensitive dye accumulation and release enables to estimate the modulation of the quantal neurotransmitter release by neuroactive substances. The action of antiepileptic drugs at the different stages of exocytosis was studied in our previous research [21]. Levetiracetam is a specific modulator of synaptic vesicle glycoprotein SV2A, whereas ethosuximide is a blocker of T-type Ca2+-channels. The estimates made by the registration of AO release suggest different modes of action of these substances on the exocytotic peak (Fig. 3A). The amplitude of exocytosis in preparations of primary neurons was estimated in % from accumulated probe (in Ft/F0). As shown in Fig. 3A exocytotic peak in control preparations of primary neurons was 28.6 ± 3.1% (mean ± SD, n = 7) from accumulated dye and decreased to 16.4 ± 2.2% after preincubation with antiepileptic drug ethosuximide (1 mM). In contrast, levetiracetam increased the amplitude of exocytosis to 44.6 ± 5.2% from accumulated AO.

Synaptic vesicle glycoprotein SV2 is involved in SV recycling. The expression of SV2A in primary hippocampal neurons was demonstrated by immunocytochemistry (Fig. 3B) and also can be qualitatively analyzed in neurons by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) analysis after intracellular staining (see Methods).

We found that levetiracetam increases the SV docking after both acute application and per oral administration: the percent of docked SV increases by 7–10% with concomitant reduction of the number of single vesicles (Fig. 3C).

We next comparatively estimated the high-K+ stimulated secretion in cortical and hippocampal neurons of animals treated with levetiracetam (Fig. 3D). Levetiracetam administration caused the rise in the amplitude of stimulated secretion in hippocampus, but for cortex no significant difference was observed (Fig. 3D). As the fast initial release of SVs depends on the number of SVs docked/primed for fusion we estimated the size of RRP in hippocampal neurons. Solutions of high osmolarity trigger Ca2+-independent exocytosis of the RRP: the resulting water efflux creates negative pressure that draws docked vesicles closer to the plasma membrane, triggering SNARE firing [22]. In animal model levetiracetam treatment was correlated with the increased size of RRP in hippocampal neurons (Fig. 3E).

This mechanism may underlie the augmentation of stimulated [14C]GABA but not glutamate release (Fig. 3F). Although levetiracetam-mediated increase in the AO secretion was typical for both cortical and hippocampal neurons in culture, in animal model we only revealed the significant changes in stimulated secretion from hippocampal neurons (Fig. 3D).

SV2 seems to regulate specifically and be of critical importance for inhibitory GABA release in the hippocampus. Delay in SV2A expression in hippocampus is associated with the epileptogenesis in SV2A−/− animals at early postnatal age and supports the hypothesis that SV2A absence favors epileptic seizures around post-natal day 7 [23]. SV2 interacts with synaptotagmin and via co-trafficking regulates the amount of synaptotagmin in SVs. This results in fewer vesicles competent for Ca2+-induced fusion in synapses lacking SV2 [24]. Thus, the role of SV2 protein in the regulation of SV release is complex, depends on the type of synapse and can be mediated by the direct interactions with SNARE-proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impairments of synaptic vesicle docking and fusion under vitamin D3 deficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Significance of vitamin D3 for normal cell function is due to its involvement in the regulation of not only Ca2+ and phosphorus homeostasis, but also of lipid metabolism, cell proliferation, differentiation, apoptosis, genome stability and neurogenesis [25]. Recent data suggests the important role of active vitamin D3 metabolite 1,25-OH D3 in regulation of neurosecretion, brain development and cognition. Neuroprotective effect of vitamin D3 is associated with its influence on neurotransmitter synthesis, intracellular calcium homeostasis and prevention of oxidative tissue damage [26]. We utilized the model of experimental VDD to study the steps of exocytosis affected by this pathology. Vitamin D3 deficiency was established by the activity of alkaline phosphatase, the level of active cholecalciferol metabolite 25-OH D3 and concentrations of Ca2+ and phosphorus in blood serum (Fig. 4B).

Upon depolarization of plasma membrane synaptosomes of VDD animals (Fig. 4A, red trace) display the reduction of exocytotic release of SV constituents without detectable changes in dynamics of endocytosis [27].

We found that SV docking is significantly attenuated under VDD with concomitant increase of single SV pool (Fig. 4C). Under VDD the portion of vesicles not involved in docking was higher and the number of docked SVs (500 nm in diameter) was lowered by 47.3% in average. Moreover, the resulting mean size of docked SV cluster was 700 nm for control animal and 450 nm for VDD rats. It is notable that VDD is characterized by the increase of cholesterol level in both synaptic plasma membranes (by 27%) and SVs (by 19%, Fig. 4B), which apparently affects the fusogenic properties of lipid bilayers.

R18 fluorescence assay is a useful tool to examine the fusion of SV membranes in cell-free system. Technique is based on R18 probe dequenching after fusion of R18-labeled and unlabeled lipid bilayers. Membrane fusion at these conditions is triggered by the application of Ca2+ (10 μM). The unspecific Ca2+-independent probe transfer consisted of 1–3% for 4 min and was subtracted from experimental traces.

Under VDD the level of Ca2+-stimulated fusion between the membranes of SVs (homotypic fusion) for 4 min was decreased by 36.7% compared to control (Fig. 4D). Thus, the revealed attenuation of SV docking and fusion underlies the decrease of the amplitude of exocytotic in presynaptic terminals (Fig. 4A).

R18 assay was also applied for confocal imaging of fused SVs. Docked SVs (unlabeled and labeled with R18 in self-quenching concentration) in the medium of synaptosomal cytosolic proteins (Fig. 4D) are invisible in the specimen. The appearance of luminous structures after application of 10−5 M Ca2+ evidences for the SV fusion and R18 dequenching as a result of lipid bilayers intermixing (principle underlying the spectrofluorimetry registration of SV fusion kinetics).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FACS analysis of surface and intracellular neuron-specific proteins
ce:section.ce:para: For a range of neuronal proteins the expression can be estimated quantitatively using conjugated antibodies or two-step labeling. After isolation of primary cells from the brain the populations of astrocytes, neurons, oligodendrocytes and microglia can be separately studied based on labeling with cell-specific markers. Although tubulins form microtubule network and are present in wide range of cells, beta tubulin III is specifically detected in neurons and represents a good tool to focus on this population (Fig. 5 ). Isotype control (in our case Mouse IgG2a, κ) should be used for screening.

Using the staining with Alexa Fluor® 488 anti-Tubulin beta III one can focus on the population of neurons, apply antibodies against the protein of interest and quantitatively analyze the expression of SNARE-proteins, neurotransmitter receptors, transporters, adaptor and other proteins in neurons in animal models. In contrast to immunocytochemistry (Fig. 3B), being a quantitative analysis FACS allows detecting much smaller differences (as significant) in surface and intracellular protein expression between groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: This paper proposes the approaches for characterization of SVs endocytosis and separate steps of exocytosis using primary cortical neurons, highly purified SVs and animal models: 1) administration of antiepileptic drug levetiracetam and 2) experimental vitamin D3 deficiency. Electron microscopy, confocal imaging, dynamic light scattering and spectrofluorimetry were combined for the reconstruction and quantitative analysis of neurotransmitter release (Fig. 6 ).

CNDs are high-performance, nontoxic fluorescence agents for bioimaging. We chose CNDs as extremely bright and photostable artificial cargo with superior physical features suitable for the neuroscience research. High resolution images of synaptosomes after loading with CNDs and following stimulated CND release (Fig. 1C and D, respectively) together with inhibition of CND loading by dynasore (Fig. 1F) demonstrated that after stimulation of endocytosis CNDs are trapped in newly formed vesicles.
Upon high-K+ depolarization loaded CNDs are released from nerve terminals via activity-driven exocytosis. In our case CNDs are likely released during full collapse fusion.

Flow cytometry of hippocampal neurons (Fig. 1F) allowed us to confirm that the first step of CND interaction with the cell is their adsorption on the plasma membrane and subsequent stimulated CND uptake into neurons occurred via SV endocytosis. We do not exclude that part of the CNDs may finally appear not only in SVs but also in lysosomes (especially when the time of incubation with cells increase).

The application of AO made possible monitoring of SV acidification, the dynamics of exo- and endocytosis and their ΔpH dependence [28, 29]. The quantal size and release probability may depend on ΔpH of SVs [30]. The remarkable acidification of both synaptosomal and isolated SVs and prominent exocytotic peak after stimulation of synaptosomes/neurons were the mandatory criteria for further experimental work.

Specific modulator of synaptic vesicle glycoprotein SV2, levetiracetam [31] and blocker of T-type Ca2+-channels, ethosuximide [32] have diverse mechanisms of antiepileptic action. We revealed that ethosuximide attenuated first phase of synchronous exocytosis and abolished the subsequent asynchronous exocytosis (Fig. 3A), whereas levetiracetam significantly increased the intensity of exocytosis. It should be noted that the exocytotic release was registered on fully differentiated primary neurons (14 DIC) with neurite outgrowth and prominent expression of SV2A protein as target for levetiracetam (Fig. 3B with 3D reconstruction in interactive data visualizations).

Electron micrographs and dynamic light scattering of the vesicle preparation confirmed that SV clustering was observed in the presence of synaptosomal cytosolic fraction (Fig. 2B–D). Neuroactive drugs acting on docking step may impact the RRP size, SV release probability or induce the disintegration of docked SVs. We demonstrated that levetiracetam increases the docking of SVs [33], while Latrodectus venom completely abolished the pre-docked SV clusters [34].

Levetiracetam, by increasing SV docking and RRP size (Fig. 3C and E) enhanced the stimulated secretion of GABA in hippocampal neurons (Fig. 3F) Findings suggest that SV2A protein is implicated in regulation of RRP through impact on the number of docked/primed SVs [35].

The loading of FM1-43 into primary cortical neurons was used for monitoring the recycling of SVs inside the living cells and quantitative analysis of SV release upon stimulation. This experimental approach is employed to determine which of many synapses along the axon is active [36]. Long-term confocal imaging of FM1-43 fluorescence in living neurons allows estimation of the intensity of vesicle turnover. The rate and the final level of FM1-43 destaining upon depolarization of plasma membrane in Ca2+-supplemented medium can be estimated by spectrofluorimentry: the plateau level of FM1-43 destaining was typically 73.4 ± 3.2% (mean ± SD, n = 5) for 5 min. Among the approaches for tracking SV recycling CNDs have advantages of higher photostability and quantum yield. The disadvantage of FM1-43 and other styryl dyes is the intensive staining of plasma membrane even after washing and repeated cycles of stimulated endocytosis. Obtained results suggest that both CNDs and FM1-43 dye can be applied for visualization of SVs by confocal imaging.

Monitoring AO dynamics in preparations of living neurons or isolated nerve terminals enabled us to detect changes in the process of exo−/endocytosis under pathological conditions. Vitamin D3 deficiency is accompanied by the dysfunction of synaptic transmission via impairments of calcium signaling and homeostasis, synaptic plasticity, and survival/differentiation of neurons [26]. Previous data demonstrated the link between VDD and several neurological disorders or defined genes regulated by active form of vitamin D3 (1,25-OH D3) without clarifying the specific mechanisms underlying functional impairments in neurosecretion. We found the differences in amplitude of exocytosis in synaptosomes of control and VDD animal (Fig. 3A). Unlike in vitro modulated neurotransmission (Fig. 3A) the changes in the amplitude of exocytosis in vivo under VDD (Fig. 4A) or other pathologies are limited to level that does not prevent animals from retaining their consciousness and motor activities.

In our cell-free system the SV fusion was registered only after application of Ca2+, which regulates the intensity of homo- and heterotypic fusion [37, 38]. Homotypic membrane fusion was attenuated under VDD (Fig. 4D) due the functional impairments at both docking step and membrane fusion itself. Increased cholesterol level in SVs and synaptic plasma membranes strongly affects the fusogenic properties of lipid bilayer and the activity of SNARE-protein complex. Taking into the account the increasing impact of vitamin D3 deficiency on progression of neurological disorders worldwide the studies of cellular mechanisms of these impairments are of great value for modern neuroscience and medicine. Moreover, our approaches are useful for the studies of other neuropathologies associated with the alterations in excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmission [39].

Among methods applied for the analysis of exocytosis and not discussed here are: 1) measurements of intracellular Ca2+ and Na+ fluxes during the first critical steps of triggering the Ca2+ transients; 2) modulation of the activity of neurotransmitter transporters/receptors and the studies of the neurotoxin action. These approaches estimate the resulting excitatory/inhibitory neurotransmission and are more advantage as functional analysis, but give poor spatial and temporal characterization of the processes within presynapse; 3) super-resolution microscopy with the detection of exchange between SV pools and imaging of proteins and lipids (STED, PALM/STORM). Although there are many interesting up to date studies of SV recycling with these techniques they often leave the question open about functional outcomes (balance of inhibitory/excitatory neurotransmission under certain influence, i. e. levetiracetam action); 4) live cell imaging of fusion/SNARE-protein interactions (FRET, FLIM) and, in the near future, in vivo imaging.

Together with the direct application of developed techniques for the functional analysis of neurotransmitter release in neurons, proposed protocols is relevant for studying exo−/endocytosis and pre-clinical testing of the neuroactive efficiency of pre-selected substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: The salts for Krebs-Ringer solution, Tris, HEPES, EGTA, EDTA, FBS, poly-d-lysine, sucrose, d-glucose, trypsin and DNAse I were from Sigma (USA). Acridine orange (AO), R18 (octadecyl rhodamine B chloride) and FM1-43 were purchased from Molecular Probe (Eugene, OR, USA). Anti-SV2A-mAb was purchased from Invitrogen. Octaethyleneglycol-dodecyl ether (C12E8) was purchased from Calbiochem (USA). All chemicals used in the research were of analytical grade. Dilutions were made by deionized water.

CNDs were synthesized and characterized in Laboratory of Nanobiotechnology, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry [14]: CNDs were obtained from a mixture of sucrose and polyethylene glycol in aqueous solution. Specifically, 4 g sucrose was mixed with 0.5 g PEG-150 and diluted in 26 mL distilled water. After that the sample was treated in a MW oven (600 W, 4 min), and 30 mL water was added, diluting the sample. As particles tend to associate additional ultrasonic treatment (22 kHz, 2 min) was used in experiments. Synthesized CNDs were water-soluble due to the surface hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups [40]. The diameter of CNDs estimated by dynamic light scattering was 8.2 ± 0.9 (mean ± SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics statement
ce:section.ce:para: The study was performed in accordance with the guidelines of the Animal Care and Use Committee of Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, NAS of Ukraine (protocol N1 from 13.01.2014) and EU (86/609/EEC). Wistar rats were kept in a housing facility for a 1-week acclimation period before experiment, with free access to water and food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animal models
ce:section.ce:para:
The animals were kept at 22 °C with a 12-h light/12-h dark cycles.
1.
Levetiracetam was administered per os to Wistar rats (6-month-old, males) at 60 mg/kg once a day. Control group received equivalent volume (100 μL) of saline. Animals were sacrificed at 15th day of experiment.
2.
Wistar rats (1-month-old, males) were kept either on standard diet with vitamin D3 supplementation (40 IU per day, control group) or vitamin D3 restricted feed with limited exposure to UV radiation (vitamin D3 deficient group, VDD) for 60 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of synaptosomes preparation from rat brain
ce:section.ce:para: Purified synaptosomes were prepared from Wistar rat (6-month-old, males) brains by Ficoll-400 density gradient centrifugation of crude preparations by Cotman [41]. Synaptosomes were used for 2–4 h post-isolation. The standard Krebs-Ringer solution contained (mM): NaCl, 126; KCl, 5; CaCl2, 2; MgCl2, 2; NaH2PO4, 1.0; HEPES, 20; d-glucose, 10.
In separate experiments synaptosomes were isolated from cerebral hemispheres of vitamin D3 deficient rats.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of synaptic vesicles and cytosolic fraction of synaptosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Synaptic vesicles from brain hemispheres were isolated according to [42]. Crude synaptosomes were lysed by rapid resuspension in 1 mM EGTA, 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.1 (3 mL/g of brain tissue) and incubated at 4 °C for 60 min. The preparation was then centrifuged at 20,000 ×g for 30 min. The supernatant was centrifuged again at 55,000 ×g for 60 min. The last centrifugation (130,000 ×g, at 4 °C, 60 min) was to obtain the SVs fraction (pellet) and the fraction of cytosolic synaptosomal proteins (supernatant). The resulting SV pellet was resuspended in intracellular solution with 10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.5. The purity of vesicle fraction was controlled by plasma membranes marker (Na+/K+-ATPase). No plasma membranes contamination was present in SV preparation after purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Primary cultures of cortical neurons
ce:section.ce:para: Cortices of E18 mouse pups were microdissected and incubated with trypsin and DNAse I for 15 min at 37 °C. Washed cells were incubated with trypsin inhibitor in the minimum essential medium (10% FBS) for 15 min. Single cell suspension was obtained by resulting mass trituration and passing through 40-μm nylon mesh cell strainer. Cells assayed by trypan blue exclusion for count and viability were washed and resuspended in plating media.

Astrocyte feeder layer was generated from cortices of 1-day-old mouse pups by the trypsinization and adherence-based selection. Primary neurons (1 × 106 cell/mL) were plated on poly-d-lysine-precoated plates. The preconditioned astrocyte feeder cultures were seeded on porous polyester membrane of Transwell inserts with 0.4 μm pore size. Experiments were carried out 14 days post co-culturing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy of the isolated synaptic vesicles
ce:section.ce:para: The SV fraction in the medium of 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 was passed through a glass microfiber Whatman GF/C filters. Suspensions of SV (300 μg proteins) were incubated for 10 min in the media of cytosolic fraction of synaptosomes (1.2 mg/mL proteins) or buffer solution (10 mM Tris-HCl. pH 7.4). The suspensions of SVs were centrifuged for 60 min at 130,000 ×g after incubation.

Precipitates were perfused with cacodylate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.2) containing 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 2 h. The SVs were post-fixed in 0.05 M cacodylate buffer containing 1% osmium tetroxide for 60 min, dehydrated in graded acetone and embedded in araldite. For electron microscopу (Hitachi 600, Japan) ultra thin slices were mildly stained with uranyl acetate and lead citrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering for monitoring SV clustering and CNDs sizes
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrodynamic diameters of the particles in SV suspension were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using Malvern 4700 Zetasizer-3 spectrometer (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, U.K.) equipped with helium‑neon laser “LG-111” (25 mW; wavelength 632.8 nm). The scattering was detected at the angle of 90°. Data were analyzed by Contin algorithm (Malvern Instruments), which calculates the Z-average size and polydispersity. The transforms of the intensity distribution to volume distributions was obtained by Malvern Instruments software.

This approach enabled us to extend the knowledge of the dispersive phase, except its viscosity. The diffusion velocity (Brownian motion) of the particles can be measured optically by DLS investigation. Then, the effective hydrodynamic radius can be calculated down via the diffusion coefficient by the Stokes– Einstein equation.

Vesicle suspension (50 μL, 50 μg) were injected into cuvette containing 950 μL of the solution of synaptosomal cytosolic fraction, 1 mM EGTA and 10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.1. The protein concentration of cytosolic fraction consisted of 1 mg/mL.
CNDs (200 μg/mL) size was estimated in Krebs-Ringer solution. DLS measurements were carried out at a room temperature (22–24 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: R18 assay for membrane fusion monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The R18 assay was proposed for membrane fusion monitoring and is based on the changes of fluorescence intensity profile of self-quenching probe [43]. The solution of R18 in ethanol was injected into 0.2 mL of SV suspension (1 mg/mL of protein, 10 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.1) and kept in the dark for 5 min at 37 °C. Final concentrations of the probe and ethanol were 20 μM and 0.5% (v/v), respectively. Unbound R18 was removed on a Sephadex G-75 column.

Fluorescence was monitored in spectrofluorimeter (Hitachi 650-10S) at λexcitation = 560 nm, λemission = 580 nm (slit widths of 5 and 5 nm, correspondingly) and 530 nm cut-off filter in the emission beam. The fluorescence of labeled SVs was quenched by 80–90%. At the end of each experiment C12E8 (octaethyleneglycol-dodecyl ether, 0.1% final concentration) was added to the reaction mixture and the maximum value (Fmax) of the dequenched fluorophore signal was obtained. This value, corrected for probe dilution, was considered as 100% probe dequenching. Quenching (Q) was calculated according to the Eq. (1), where F is the registered fluorescence and Fmax is the fluorescence measured after addition of detergent C12E8 (octaethyleneglycol-dodecyl ether) at a final concentration of 0.1%. R18-labeled SVs were kept on ice in the dark until use.
(1)
Q = 1 – F /
F max

We employed the R18 technique for the detection the SV fusion with each other (homotypic fusion). Fusion was registered at spectrofluorimeter Hitachi 650-10S (Japan) in suspension of R18-labeled and unlabeled SVs at a ratio of 1:8 (based on protein concentration) after Ca2+ application [8, 38]. The increase in the fluorescence signal resulting from membrane fusion and dequenching was recorded. To quantify the probe dilution during fusion of the vesicles with target membranes, the initial fluorescence signal of R18-labeled SVs (Io) was measured. The time-dependent dequenching of fluorescence signal (I) was recorded during 4 min after starting the experiment.

At the end of each experiment, detergent C12E8 at a final concentration of 0.1% was added to the reaction mixture, where the maximum value (Id) of the dequenched fluorophore signal was obtained. The rate of fusion at any time point is proportional to the percentage of fluorescence dequenching (%FD) and thus, can be defined by Eq. (2).
(2)
% FD = 100
I –
I o
/
I d
−
I o
The membrane fusion was determined by monitoring of fluorescence upon CaCl2 addition. Free Ca2+ concentrations were set by Ca2+/EGTA buffers as described by Portzehl et al. [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of stimulated uptake and release of CNDs in presynapse
ce:section.ce:para: CNDs (final concentration of 10 μg/mL) were loaded into the purified synaptosomal fraction (0.3 mg/mL of protein) during high-K+ stimulation in Ca2+-containing standard salt solution for 5 min at 30 °C [45]. Synaptosomes were washed twice with Krebs-Ringer solution.

The fluorescence of synaptic plasma membrane was detected by labeling with R18 probe. Based on dependence of R18 fluorescence quenching on the concentration of the probe in membrane [38] we choose the R18 concentration (1 nmol/240 μg of synaptosomal protein) that does not cause prominent quenching of the probe and enables to visualize the membrane.

Confocal imaging after loading and after high-K+-stimulated release was made using LSM 510 META module, Carl Zeiss, objective Plan-Apochromat 100×/1.4 Oil DIC. The excitations for R18 and CNDs were 543 nm and 405 nm, respectively. Specimens for confocal imaging were imaged with sequential Multi-track acquisition mode, which allows separate exciting and registering each dye/emitter and avoiding cross-emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of endocytotic CND loading in hippocampal neurons
ce:section.ce:para: Flow cytometry was performed on Beckman COULTER®EPICS™XL™. CNDs were applied to neurons without stimulation of endocytosis (adsorption) or after high-K+ application as described in previous section.

To verify if CND loading during high-K+ stimulation occurred via endocytosis we employed dynasore (80 μM, 0.4% DMSO), a cell-permeable inhibitor of dynamin-dependent endocytosis, or 0.4% DMSO (standard loading) 5 min before CND application.
Measurements were based on the count of 30,000 events and allow fast population-based quantitative estimation of CND loading. Fluorescence peaks were overlaid for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal imaging of stimulated SV fusion in preformed clusters
ce:section.ce:para: Isolated SVs (1 mg/mL of protein) were labeled with R18 probe at self-quenching concentration as described in R18 assay (final concentrations of 20 μM). The fusion of SVs was registered between labeled and unlabeled SVs at a ratio of 1:8 in the presence of cytosolic synaptosomal proteins. Fusion of SV membrane bilayers in preformed clusters was visualized as appearance of fluorescent structures upon Ca2+ application.
Confocal imaging was made using LSM 510 META module, Carl Zeiss, objective Plan-Apochromat 100×/1.4 Oil DIC. The excitation for R18 was 543 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of SV2A: immunocytochemistry on primary hippocampal neurons
ce:section.ce:para: For labeling with anti-SV2A primary cultures of hippocampal neurons were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and washed with PBS twice. After blocking of nonspecific sites and incubation (1 h, 20–24 °C) with primary antibodies (anti-SV2A, 8 μg/mL) washed samples were kept with secondary antibodies (Alexa Fluor 555, 0.2 μg/mL) for another 1 h. Nucleus was stained with DAPI (0.1 μg/mL) for 5 min (20–24 °C). Specimens were visualized by Zeiss LSM780 Microscope, Plan Apochromat 40×/1.4 Oil DIC.

3D reconstruction (interactive data visualizations) was performed after deconvolution of slices. The stacks of optical sections (DAPI and Alexa 555) were up-sampled (3×) and subjected to blind deconvolution (5 iterations for DAPI and 25 for Alexa) using Huyghens v. 3.7 (SVI, The Netherlands). The processing was initialized with the nominal PSF (maximum emission wavelength and objective NA = 1.4) and performed at SNR set to 10 (Alexa) and 15 (DAPI), respectively. The background was estimated as a minimum of average intensity in 25 × 25 region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Registration of the synaptic vesicles acidification and exocytotic release
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in the acidification of SVs were registered with pH-sensitive fluorescent dye acridine orange (AO). Acridine orange is a pH-sensitive lipophilic fluorescent dye capable of permeating cell and organelle membrane structure. It freely enters the cytoplasm and tends to form dimers and multimers after protonation at organelles with acidic pH [30, 46]. Then, it becomes membrane impermeable and become trapped in SVs. This is registered (Figs. 3A and 4A, D) as decrease of green fluorescence inherent for monomeric dye. Multimers of AO have red fluorescence that enables to visualize acidic organelles (with resolution limitations) by confocal imaging. Although AO is accumulated in all acidic compartments of the neurons (mainly in SVs, but also in lysosomes) recycling of the dye in presynapse is predominantly related to the turnover of SVs.

Isolated SVs at a final protein concentration in the intracellular solution of 0.3 mg/mL or synaptosomes at a final protein concentration in Krebs-Ringer solution of 0.2 mg/mL were preincubated at 37 °C for 10 min and fluorescence measurements were performed at spectrofluorimeter Hitachi 650-10S (Japan) at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 490 and 530 nm (2 nm slit), correspondingly. High-K+ depolarization of plasma membrane was achieved in Ca2+ containing medium by partial replacement of Na+ by K+ (35 mM K+) at the steady-state of AO fluorescence. RRP size was estimated using hypertonic sucrose application (0.5 M). The traces were normalized to the similar data at the absence of SVs/synaptosomes by the Eq. (3).
(3)
F =
F t
/
F 0
where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of AO quenching in the solution, and Ft was the fluorescence intensity of AO in the presence of isolated synaptic vesicles or synaptosomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of GABA release
ce:section.ce:para: Release of GABA was estimated from [14C]GABA-loaded neurons of cortex and hippocampus. [14C]GABA (final concentration of 20 nM) was applied to neurons in Ca2+-supplemented media for 20 min at 35 °C and the preparations were washed with Krebs-Ringer solution twice and supplemented from this point onwards with 100 μM amino-oxyacetic acid (to inhibit GABA transaminase), constantly aerated and maintained at 30 °C. The release was stimulated by high-K+ depolarization and the fractions were analyzed for [14C]GABA content by liquid scintillation counting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FM1-43 staining of recycling synaptic vesicles
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent styryl dye FM1-43 was applied to monitor the recycling and release of synaptic vesicles in primary neurons. This probe binds to plasma membrane and labels newly formed endosomes/recycled synaptic vesicles after high-K+ stimulated loading (endocytosis). To depolarize the synaptic plasma membrane in Ca2+-supplemented medium the isosmotic substitution of Na+ for K+ was performed resulting in final K+ concentration of 35 mM. Cells were incubated with FM1-43 (5 μM) for 20 min before being washed twice. To exclude the impact of spontaneous exocytosis the steady-state level of fluorescence was registered by spectrofluorimetry before stimulation. FM1-43 destaining was calculated in percentage of total accumulated dye [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) analysis: intracellular staining
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse cortical and hippocampal cells were isolated in Accutase for 30 min at 4 °C. Tubes were centrifuged at 425 ×g for 2 min, pellet was resuspended ice-cold DMEM. Samples were triturated 10 times with a large diameter fire-polished Pasteur pipette (1.3 mm). Large debris and cell clusters were removed from the cell suspension by serial filtration through pre-wetted 100 μm and then 40 μm cell strainers. Cells were centrifuged at 400 ×g for 7 min and resuspended in FACS staining buffer (PBS-/5% FBS).

Cells were treated with FIX&PERM (Nordic MUBio), and then stained with TubIIIβ (clone AA10) Alexa Fluor® 488 (filled histogram, Biolegend) or mouse IgG2a, κ Alexa Fluor® 488 isotype control (open histogram, Biolegend) for 30 min. Samples were analyzed on a BD FACSCanto II.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Values are presented as means with standard deviation (SD) for n independent experiments. The number of tested samples (n) is specified in figure legends. All samples were prepared in triplicate for each analysis by spectrofluorimetry, photon correlation spectroscopy, or cholesterol content determination. The absence of prominent exocytotic peak (25–30% from accumulated dye, inclusion criterion) typical for functional synaptosomes and neurons was the criterion for data exclusion.

Relative quantitation of confocal images was performed via comparing the signal intensity between regions of interest and between the sample and a control using constant acquisition settings. The grayscale indication was used to avoid oversaturation of the pixels.

For VDD animal model rats were randomized based on mean value of body weight. The maximum mean difference was below 1%. The significant difference between the populations of single SVs and SVs clusters was detected by one-way ANOVA at the 0.05 significance level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acknowledgements
ce:section.ce:para: We would like to thank Alexander Demchenko (Head of the Laboratory of Nanobiotechnology, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry) for helpful advices regarding experiments with CNDs, and Mariia Dekaliuk for synthesis of CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conflicts of interests
ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no actual or potential conflicts of interests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Author contributions
ce:section.ce:para: Ludmila Kasatkina: measured the fluorescence profiles (AO, FM1-43 destaining, flow cytometry), GABA release, handled cell cultures, analyzed the results and wrote the manuscript. Vitaliy Gumenyuk: isolated the synaptosomes and SVs, registered SV fusion and clustering, estimated the loading and release of CNDs. Eva Sturm and Akos Heinemann: measured the expression of intracellular neuronal marker by FACS analysis, analyzed the results. Tytus Bernas: performed the deconvolution and 3D reconstruction (staining with anti-SV2A). Irene Trikash: designed the study, developed the cell-free system for studying the Ca2+-dependent fusion of SVs with target membranes, registered and evaluated SV fusion and clustering in cell-free system, estimated the loading and release of CNDs, analyzed the results and wrote the manuscript. All authors reviewed the results and approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Funding
ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported in part by OPTEC's grant program (project No. 6-13, 2013), by grant No. F63/41-2016 of the State Fund for Fundamental Research of Ukraine (SFFRU) and by joint Ukraine-Austria R&D project (No. UA 09/2016).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Axonal transport: cargo-specific mechanisms of motility and regulation
Molecular anatomy of a trafficking organelle
Evidence for recycling of synaptic vesicle membrane during transmitter release at the frog neuromuscular junction
A nanoscale resolution view on synaptic vesicle dynamics
The structural organization of the readily releasable pool of synaptic vesicles

sb:author.ce:surname: De - P, Niemann H, And Jahn R.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Membrane fusion machinery: insights from synaptic proteins
Fusion of isolated synaptic vesicles as a model of the terminal stage of regulated exocytosis
Docking and fusion of synaptic vesicles in cell-free model system of exocytosis
Activity-dependent fluorescent staining and destaining of living motor nerve terminals
Single synaptic vesicles fusing transiently and successively without loss of identity
Using the fluorescent styryl dye FM1-43 to visualize synaptic vesicles exocytosis and endocytosis in motor nerve terminals
Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials: "quantum dots" or nanoclusters?
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Photoluminescence of carbon Nanodots: dipole emission centers and Electron–phonon coupling
Synaptic vesicle dynamics in living cultured hippocampal neurons visualized with CY3-conjugated antibodies directed against the lumenal domain of synaptotagmin
Synchronous and asynchronous exocytosis induced by subthreshold high K+ at Cs(+)-loaded terminals of rat hippocampal neurons
Effects of antiepileptic agents on homotypic fusion of synaptic vesicles
Definition of the readily releasable pool of vesicles at hippocampal synapses
Expression of SV2 isoforms during rodent brain development
Co-trafficking of SV2 and synaptotagmin at the synapse
Vitamin D and the central nervous system
Altered cholesterol level and synaptic vesicle fusion implicated in impaired neurotransmission under vitamin D3-deficiency
4-Аminopyridine sequesters intracellular Ca2+ which triggers exocytosis in excitable and non-excitable cells
The pH-sensitive dye acridine orange as a tool to monitor exocytosis/endocytosis in synaptosomes
Stimulation-dependent regulation of the pH, volume and quantal size of bovine and rodent secretory vesicles
The synaptic vesicle protein SV2A is the binding site for the antiepileptic drug levetiracetam
Block of T-type Ca2+ channels is an important action of Succinimide Antiabsence drugs
Effects of Levetiracetam on aggregation and fusion of membranes of synaptic vesicles in a cell-free exocytosis model
The phenomenon of synaptic vesicle clustering as the prefusion state in the model system of exocytosis
Modulation of neurosecretion by levetiracetam and approaches for its multistep analysis
Fusion of synaptic vesicles and plasma membrane in the presence of synaptosomal soluble proteins
The fusion of synaptic vesicle membranes studied by lipid mixing: the R18 fluorescence assay validity
Diabetes-induced impairments of the exocytosis process and the effect of gabapentin: the link with cholesterol level in neuronal plasma membranes
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Isolation of synaptosomal and synaptic plasma membrane fractions
Calcium dependent neurotransmitter release and protein phosphorylation in synaptic vesicles
Fluorescence method for measuring the kinetics of fusion between biological membranes
The dependence of concentration and relaxation of muscle fibres from the crab Maia squinado of the internal concentration of free calcium ions
Synaptosomes as a platform for loading nanoparticles into synaptic vesicles
New insights into molecular mechanism(s) underlying the presynaptic action of nitric oxide on GABA release
